stage.name: Blockchain Foundations
stage.description: SHA-256, Merkle trees, Proof-of-Work vs Proof-of-Stake, Byzantine fault tolerance, and the blockchain trilemma.

elements.content: What Is a Blockchain?
A cryptographically linked, append-only distributed ledger

17K+
Nodes

600 GB
Chain size

The Core Structure

Each block contains:
• Previous block's hash (linking the chain)
• Merkle root of all transactions
• Timestamp + nonce

Changing block N invalidates every block after it — an attacker must redo all downstream proof-of-work.

Why It Matters

Bitcoin: ~17,000 nodes worldwide
No CEO, no server to shut down

Throughput reality:
• Bitcoin: 7 tx/s
• Ethereum L1: ~15 tx/s
• Visa: ~24,000 tx/s

This gap is why Layer 2 networks exist.

actions.text: Welcome to Blockchain Basics. In this first lesson we're asking: what exactly IS a blockchain?
A blockchain is a cryptographically linked, append-only distributed ledger — let's break each of those words down.
The core structure: every block contains the previous block's hash — that's the cryptographic link — plus a Merkle root representing all the transactions inside it.
And why does this matter? Bitcoin alone has 17 thousand nodes worldwide. There is no CEO, no server you can shut down. The network is the bank.
Over 17,000 nodes secure the Bitcoin network right now.
The Bitcoin chain is over 600 gigabytes and still growing — that's the full history of every transaction since 2009.

elements.content: Cryptographic Hash Functions
The one-way mathematical trap that makes blockchain tamper-proof
SHA-256(input) → 256-bit digest

Pre-image resistant · Collision resistant · Avalanche effect

Change one character → ~50% of output bits flip
This is why any tampering is immediately detectable
Quantum note: Grover's algorithm halves the effective security — 256-bit SHA-256 gives 128-bit quantum security, still considered safe.

actions.text: Cryptographic hash functions are the foundation of blockchain security.
SHA-256 is a one-way mathematical trap — easy to compute forward, essentially impossible to reverse.
SHA-256 takes any input and produces a fixed 256-bit digest. Same input always gives the same output. Different input gives a completely different output.

Three key properties: pre-image resistant — you can't reverse it. Collision resistant — two different inputs don't give the same hash. And the avalanche effect — change one character and about 50% of the output bits flip.

One important note for the future: quantum computers running Grover's algorithm would effectively halve the security of SHA-256. That's why 256-bit gives 128-bit quantum security — still considered safe.

elements.content: Merkle Trees — Efficient Verification
Prove a transaction exists with just a few hashes

log₂N
Proof size
1

Transactions

1,000s of transactions in a block — too large to transmit fully
2

Pair & Hash

Hash pairs repeatedly up a binary tree until one root hash remains
3

Merkle Root

Only 32 bytes stored in the block header — represents all transactions
4

Proof Path

Prove any tx is included with just log₂(N) hashes — no full block needed

actions.text: Merkle trees solve a critical problem: how do you prove a transaction is in a block without downloading the entire block?
With just a few hashes — specifically log base 2 of N hashes — you can prove any transaction's inclusion.
Step one: a block can contain thousands of transactions. Way too large to transmit entirely to every device.
Step two: hash pairs of transactions repeatedly until you're left with a single root hash — the Merkle root.
Step three: only 32 bytes — the Merkle root — gets stored in the block header. It represents ALL transactions.
Step four: to prove your transaction is included, you only need log N hashes along the path to the root. For a million transactions, that's just 20 hashes.

elements.content: Consensus: Proof-of-Work vs Proof-of-Stake
Two fundamentally different answers to the same question

⛏ Proof of Work

• Energy = security cost (unforgeable)

• Miners compete on hash rate

• Difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks

• Attack cost = 51% of global hashrate

• 700 exahashes/sec on Bitcoin

• Proven 15-year security record
VS

🪙 Proof of Stake

• Collateral = security cost

• Validators chosen by stake weight

• Slashing destroys dishonest stake

• Attack cost = 1/3 of all staked ETH

• ~$24B at stake on Ethereum

• 90%+ energy reduction vs PoW

actions.text: How does a blockchain network agree on what happened? That's the consensus problem.
Two dominant approaches: Proof of Work and Proof of Stake. Fundamentally different answers to the same trust question.
Proof of Work: energy is the security cost. You can't fake burning electricity. Miners compete on hash rate, and difficulty adjusts every two weeks to maintain 10-minute blocks.
Proof of Stake: collateral replaces energy. Validators stake ETH and can be 'slashed' — lose their stake — if they try to cheat. The economic incentive IS the security.

elements.content: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
How blockchains achieve agreement despite dishonest participants

80%
XRPL threshold
Need: 3f + 1 honest nodes to tolerate f traitors

With 1 traitor you need 4 total — with 100 traitors you need 301 total

Bitcoin solves this probabilistically with PoW
XRPL solves it with 80% trusted-validator threshold
Named after the 1982 Lamport, Shostak & Pease paper. Every blockchain consensus is solving a variant of this problem.

actions.text: Byzantine Fault Tolerance asks: how many dishonest participants can a network tolerate before it breaks?
Named after a famous 1982 computer science paper — every blockchain consensus mechanism is solving a version of this problem.
The math: to tolerate F traitors, you need 3F plus 1 honest nodes. One bad actor? You need 4 total. 100 bad actors? You need 301.
Bitcoin solves this probabilistically through hash power — making it economically irrational to attack. XRPL uses a different approach with trusted validator lists.
XRPL requires 80% of trusted validators to agree before a ledger closes. This is higher than Bitcoin's 51% threshold.

elements.content: The Blockchain Trilemma
Every blockchain picks two — no one has solved all three yet

🔒 Security

Resistant to 51% attacks, double-spend, Sybil attacks. Bitcoin and Ethereum both prioritize this — compromise here risks everything.

⚡ Scalability

High transactions per second with low fees. Achieved via sharding, rollups, or larger blocks. Ethereum targets 100K+ tx/s via L2 rollups.

🌐 Decentralization

No single control point. More nodes = more resilient. Bitcoin's 17K+ nodes make it uncensorable — but slower.

actions.text: The blockchain trilemma is one of the most important concepts in the space. Every blockchain picks two out of three — and no one has fully solved all three yet.
Security, scalability, and decentralization — the impossible triangle.
Security: resistant to 51% attacks, double-spends, and Sybil attacks. Bitcoin and Ethereum both prioritize this above the other two.
Scalability: high transactions per second with low fees. Achieved via sharding, rollups, or simply larger blocks — each comes with trade-offs.
Decentralization: no single control point. More nodes equals more resilience. Bitcoin's 17 thousand nodes make it essentially uncensorable — but slow.

elements.content: Smart Contracts & the EVM
Self-executing code that no one can stop — or fix

What Is a Smart Contract?

Code deployed to Ethereum that runs exactly as written — forever. No CEO can modify it. No server can shut it down.

Once deployed, the contract is immutable. This is both the power and the danger.

The DAO hack (2016): $60M stolen from immutable code.

The Gas System

Every computation costs gas — prevents infinite loops and DoS.

EIP-1559 (Aug 2021):
• Base fee burned permanently
• 3.5M+ ETH destroyed since launch
• ETH becomes deflationary under high demand

Gas price = Base Fee + Priority Tip

actions.text: Smart contracts are self-executing code deployed to Ethereum that runs exactly as written — forever.
No CEO can change them, no government can stop them. That's both the power and the risk.
What is a smart contract? It's code on the Ethereum blockchain. Once deployed, it runs autonomously. If the code has a bug — like the DAO hack in 2016 — there's no customer support line.

The gas system is Ethereum's metering mechanism. Every computation costs gas — this prevents infinite loops and denial-of-service attacks. EIP-1559 introduced a base fee that gets burned, making ETH deflationary during high usage.

content.html:
SHA-256 Avalanche Effect Explorer

Change one character — watch ~50% of hash bits flip instantly.

—
—

A cryptographic hash must change ~50% of output bits for any single-bit input change. This makes tampering immediately detectable.

actions.text: Let's explore this interactively. Use the simulation below to experiment with the concepts we just covered.
Try different inputs and see how the outputs change. Hands-on exploration builds intuition faster than reading.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Merkle root in a block header?

options.label: Efficiently verify any transaction with minimal data

questions.analysis: A Merkle root lets you prove a transaction is included using only log₂(N) hashes — no need to download the full block.

questions.question: Bitcoin processes approximately how many transactions per second on Layer 1?
questions.analysis: Bitcoin L1 handles ~7 tx/s vs Visa's ~24,000 tx/s. This throughput gap is what drives Layer 2 research.

questions.question: In the Byzantine Generals Problem, what is the minimum number of generals needed to tolerate 1 traitor?
questions.analysis: You need 3f+1 total participants to handle f traitors. With f=1, that means at least 4 generals.

questions.question: Which TWO properties does Proof-of-Stake use instead of energy expenditure?
questions.analysis: PoS replaces energy with economic collateral and slashing — attacking costs you at least 1/3 of all staked ETH.

questions.question: Explain in 2–3 sentences why changing one transaction deep in a blockchain would require redoing enormous computational work.
questions.commentPrompt: Award full marks if the student explains: (1) changing a transaction changes the block's hash, (2) each block contains the previous block's hash so all subsequent blocks become invalid, (3) the attacker must redo proof-of-work for every block after the altered one to create a longer chain.

actions.text: Time to test your knowledge! Answer each question carefully — you can review the material after the quiz.
Take your time with these questions. The goal is to solidify your understanding, not just pass the quiz.
